reasoning: The Federal Reserve has been broadly easing monetary policy throughout late 2024 and 2025, creating a sustained downward trajectory for prevailing interest rates. The FDIC national savings rate was already as low as 0.400% by late October 2025, dropping from its prior peak. Because commercial banks are typically quick to lower deposit rates in a falling-rate environment (and generally slow to raise them when rates rise), the savings rate is overwhelmingly likely to either decrease further or remain flat over the next few months. A strict increase would likely require a substantial and unexpected reversal in monetary policy, such as sudden rate hikes driven by a renewed inflation shock, which banking institutions would still be hesitant to pass on to savers immediately. Thus, the probability of the savings rate being strictly greater than its November 9 baseline is very low. Uncertainty is slightly elevated for the later date (February 2026), reflecting a moderately longer forecast horizon where unexpected macroeconomic shifts have slightly more time to materialize.
